text:
Your working directory is a checkout of the real downshift repository at the commit where the bug below reproduces. Find and fix the bug in the library source.

Bug: in the `useCombobox` hook, when the menu is open and the user clicks one of the items to select it, the text input momentarily loses focus (a focus flicker). The item is still selected, but focus should never leave the input during a click-to-select — after clicking an item the input must remain focused (and show the selected item's value). The underlying cause is that a native mousedown on the item blurs the input before the selection is handled. The correct behavior is that `getItemProps()` returns an `onMouseDown` handler for each item that suppresses this default blur (so focus stays on the input), and this handler must compose correctly with a consumer-supplied `onMouseDown`: the consumer's handler still runs, and if the consumer marks the event with `preventDownshiftDefault` then downshift's own blur-suppression is skipped. Fix the library source so clicking an item keeps the input focused. Fix the library source (under `src/`); do not just add workarounds in test files.

Environment notes: dependencies are already installed (Node, npm). You can scope the existing Jest suite to whatever file you're iterating on with `npx kcd-scripts test --no-watch <path-to-file>`. Fix the library source; do not just add workarounds in test files.

text: The bug is isolated to the hook implementation: `useCombobox` has item click and mouse-move behavior but no item `onMouseDown`, while the class API already contains the expected pattern. I’m comparing those implementations and the existing hook tests so the fix matches local event-composition semantics.

The class implementation confirms the intended contract: compose consumer `onMouseDown` first, then call `preventDefault()`, with the shared composer stopping when `preventDownshiftDefault` is set. I’ll add that handler to `useCombobox`, including disabled items so a click cannot redirect focus, and cover both composition and opt-out behavior.

The first patch did not apply because the existing return object has slightly different indentation than the surrounding excerpt. No files were changed; I’m re-reading the exact block and will apply a narrower patch.

The source now exposes a composed `onMouseDown` for every combobox item, calls `preventDefault()` after the consumer handler, and honors the existing opt-out flag. The focused tests now assert normal suppression and consumer opt-out; I’m running them next.

text: All 10 `useCombobox` suites pass (215 tests, 9 snapshots). The implementation also preserves disabled-item focus suppression and uses the library’s existing `callAllEventHandlers`, so native and React `preventDownshiftDefault` conventions remain consistent; I’m doing the final lint/diff check now.

Fixed `useCombobox` item focus flicker.

Changes:
- Added composed `onMouseDown` to `getItemProps()`.
- Calls `event.preventDefault()` to keep focus on the input.
- Preserves consumer handlers and respects `preventDownshiftDefault`.
- Added regression tests.

Verification: all 215 `useCombobox` tests and lint pass.
